items.correct: Five distinct arguments for God's existence: from motion, causation, contingency, gradation of being, and final causes/teleology — collectively forming Aquinas's natural theology, not a single argument.
items.misconception: Aquinas's Five Ways are five steps of one cosmological argument.
items.random_wrong: Aquinas's Five Ways are five Christian sacraments.
items.why: Each Way is a self-standing argument with different structure. Students treat them as a single chain, missing that Aquinas offered multiple independent routes — the failure of one doesn't undermine the others.

items.q: What distinction matters for the free will defence to the problem of evil?
items.correct: Between moral evil (caused by free human choices) and natural evil (earthquakes, disease) — the free will defence directly addresses moral evil but struggles with natural evil, which isn't a product of free choice.
items.misconception: The free will defence solves all forms of evil at once.
items.random_wrong: Natural evil means evil that is good for nature.
items.why: Students apply the free will defence to all evil. The challenge of natural evil is precisely that no one chose it, requiring other theodicies (Augustinian fallen world, Irenaean soul-making) to address it.

items.q: What is the problem of evil?
items.correct: If God is all-loving, all-knowing and all-powerful, why does evil and suffering exist? Logically, all four cannot be true together without explanation.
items.misconception: It is the question of whether evil people will go to hell.
items.random_wrong: It is the question of whether the devil really exists.
items.why: Students treat it as a moral or eschatological question. It is actually a logical challenge to classical theism — and the central problem theodicies try to solve.

items.q: Is the Kalam argument the same as Aquinas's Third Way?
items.correct: No — Kalam argues the universe began to exist and so needs a cause; the Third Way argues from the contingency of things to a necessary being, and works even if the universe were eternal.
items.misconception: The Kalam argument and Aquinas's Third Way are the same argument with different names.
items.random_wrong: The Kalam argument is a version of the ontological argument.
items.why: Aquinas explicitly held that philosophy cannot prove the world had a beginning — he relied on contingency instead. Distinguishing temporal beginning from ontological dependence is a top-band skill.

items.q: Could Paley have responded to Darwin's theory of evolution?
items.correct: Not directly — Paley published Natural Theology in 1802 and died in 1805, over fifty years before On the Origin of Species (1859); evolution is a later challenge raised against his argument.
items.misconception: Paley wrote the watchmaker argument specifically to refute Darwin's theory of evolution.
items.random_wrong: Darwin and Paley debated each other publicly in Oxford.
items.why: The chronology matters: Darwin actually read Paley at Cambridge. Students writing that Paley 'ignored evolution' commit an anachronism that undermines their evaluation.

items.q: Aquinas' Five Ways appear in which work?
items.why: Correct answer: Summa Theologica. “Summa Contra Gentiles” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.
The answer examiners want is: Anselm of Canterbury. “Aquinas” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: In what year was Paley's Natural Theology published?
items.why: The real figure is 1802. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
